items.q: Does an interpreter produce an executable file that can be run later?
items.correct: No - an interpreter translates and executes the source code line by line each run; it is the compiler that produces a stored object-code executable.
items.misconception: Yes - interpreters save a machine-code file just like compilers.
items.random_wrong: Yes - interpreters convert programs into assembly files on disk.
items.why: Both translators produce running programs, so the artefact difference is missed. Interpretation leaves nothing behind: the source must be re-translated on every run.

items.q: Is the operating system an example of application software?
items.correct: No - the operating system is system software: it manages hardware, memory, files and processes, providing the platform on which applications run.
items.misconception: Yes - the OS is just another app installed on the computer.
items.random_wrong: Yes - anything with a user interface is application software.
items.why: The OS has menus and settings like an app, blurring the category. The test is purpose: managing the machine itself is system software; serving user tasks is application software.

items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR outputs 1 when at least one input is 1; XOR outputs 1 only when exactly one input is 1, so they differ when both inputs are 1.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with two different names.
items.random_wrong: OR works on numbers while XOR works on letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical both-true case: OR gives 1 but XOR gives 0. The 'exclusive' in XOR excludes the case where both inputs are true.

items.q: Which of these is system software?
items.why: Correct answer: An operating system. “A spreadsheet package” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the key difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
items.correct: A compiler translates the whole program before execution; an interpreter translates and executes line by line
items.misconception: An interpreter produces a standalone executable file
items.random_wrong: A compiler executes code without translating it
items.why: It's A compiler translates the whole program before execution; an interpreter translates and executes line by line. “An interpreter produces a standalone executable file” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Assembly language mnemonics into machine code
items.why: Correct answer: Assembly language mnemonics into machine code. “High-level language into pseudocode” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An AND gate outputs 1 only when:
items.why: It's Both of its inputs are 1. “At least one input is 1” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many rows are needed in a truth table for a logic circuit with 3 inputs?
items.why: The real figure is 8 rows. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
